sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a sore on his right foot that has not healed in two to three weeks, initially presenting as a blister due to tight shoes, which he has since replaced.
He has been treating it with band-aids and antibiotics, which were completed yesterday.
He denies pain, fever, chills, or drainage from the wound.
The patient has a history of neuropathy, which he attributes to the tight shoes.
He also mentions that the wound sticks to his sock when he removes it.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, with an A1c level of over 8% and has seen a podiatrist in the past but is currently without a primary care physician.
He does not mention any other medical conditions or allergies.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms, but mentions that he has had issues with stability with his shoes in the past.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: Temperature is 98.1°F, heart rate is 72, respirations are 16, blood pressure is 110/65.
Physical exam: A 1x2 inch circular wound is present on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, no necrosis, and no odor.
The vascular exam is notable for palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, with no palpable DP or PT pulses but with Doppler signs present.
Test Results: An x-ray of the right foot showed no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with compromised wound healing due to neuropathy and poor blood supply.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; compromised wound healing; neuropathy; diabetes mellitus (status: active).
Plan:
I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily, covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I ordered an ankle-brachial index (ABI) to assess the blood supply to the foot.
I will also refer the patient to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply for wound healing.
I will see the patient again in two weeks.
I plan to refer the patient to a diabetic specialist to discuss ways to manage his diabetes and bring down his A1c level.
Follow-up: I plan to see the patient again in two weeks.
